summary: This brief post documents and attributes the famous Phil Karlton quote about the two hardest problems in computer science: cache invalidation and naming things. Fowler explains he created this page because he couldn't find a satisfactory URL for the quote. The post has evolved over time to collect clever variations on the joke from various programmers on Twitter. It serves primarily as a reference page and celebration of programming humor rather than a technical analysis.
key_points: The original quote by Phil Karlton identifies cache invalidation and naming as computing's two hardest problems
The joke format has spawned numerous variations that poke fun at different programming challenges
The distributed systems variation cleverly demonstrates message ordering problems through its own structure
The earliest internet attribution traces to Tim Bray's blog around 1996-7
The meta-commentary suggests the proliferation of variations hints that programming genuinely is difficult
money_quotes: There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things.
There are 2 hard problems in computer science: cache invalidation, naming things, and off-by-1 errors.
There are only two hard problems in distributed systems: 2. Exactly-once delivery 1. Guaranteed order of messages 2. Exactly-once delivery
there's two hard problems in computer science: we only have one joke and it's not funny.
There are so many variations on the 'there are only two hard problems in computer programming...' joke that I'm starting to suspect that programming isn't actually very easy.
key_insight: The enduring popularity and endless variations of this joke reveal a truth about software development: naming things well and managing cached state remain genuinely difficult problems that every programmer encounters.
